scenario: At Meridian Robotics, a mid-size industrial automation vendor, the security team runs an internal red-team exercise using an AI-driven reconnaissance agent against their OT lab network. In under six hours the agent enumerates the PLC fleet, cracks a legacy protocol credential, and maps an exploitable path to the safety controller — a task their human red team had budgeted three days for.
question: Which MITRE framework catalogs adversary tactics and techniques against the AI/ML components enabling this kind of autonomous reconnaissance — distinct from MITRE ATT&CK for ICS, which covers the OT infrastructure itself?
options: MITRE ATT&CK for Enterprise — covers adversary tactics against standard IT infrastructure and cloud services.
MITRE ATLAS — catalogs adversary tactics and techniques against AI-enabled systems, including reconnaissance and ML attack staging.
MITRE D3FEND — a defensive countermeasure taxonomy mapped to ATT&CK techniques, not a threat catalog.
MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — covers execution, lateral movement, and impact against traditional OT infrastructure.
aria_hint: Think about which MITRE framework was built specifically for attacks against machine-learning models and pipelines, not physical OT infrastructure.
debrief: MITRE ATLAS (Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems) catalogs adversary tactics and techniques against AI-enabled systems — reconnaissance, ML attack staging, and exfiltration against models, training data, and inference logic — filling a gap ATT&CK for ICS does not cover. Independent research on the 2025 Dragos OT CTF found an AI agent reached rank 1 within 8 hours and held a 37% velocity advantage over the top human teams, underscoring why AI-specific threat modelling now matters for OT defenders. Option D (ATT&CK for ICS) maps adversary behaviour against the OT infrastructure itself — PLCs, HMIs, protocols — but has no coverage for attacks against the AI components performing reconnaissance. Option A (ATT&CK for Enterprise) covers standard IT/cloud systems, not AI-specific attack surface. Option C (D3FEND) is a defensive countermeasure taxonomy mapped to ATT&CK — useful for building defences, but not a threat catalog itself.

sources.text: Mayoral-Vilches et al., "Cybersecurity AI in OT: Insights from an AI Top-10 Ranker in the Dragos OT CTF 2025" (arXiv:2511.05119)
